Adding a resident element to a particular component causes that element to be generated within the source file.</li><li class="atl-forced-newline"><em>UML Standard Profile,</em> containing stereotypes that are necessary in order to work with the modeling tool, primitive data types and constraints (which are UML standard), and UML 2 metamodel elements. The following data types are specified in the modeling tool: boolean, byte, char, date, double, float, int, Integer, real long, short, void, and string.</li></ul><p class="atl-forced-newline">You can create your own packages for keeping model elements as well. By default, packages cannot be deleted or renamed in a project (except for the <em>File View</em> package).</p><p class="atl-forced-newline">To start a new project, first, you must create a new workspace for it. You can select a project type from the following domains:</p><ul><li class="atl-forced-newline"><strong>General-Purpose Modeling</strong> (UML, Use Case, Guide to UML Diagrams projects)</li><li class="atl-forced-newline"><strong>System Engineering</strong> (SysML v1 project)</li><li class="atl-forced-newline"><strong>Enterprise Modeling</strong> (DoDAF, DoDAF 2.0, MODAF projects)</li><li class="atl-forced-newline"><strong>Software Engineering</strong> (Scrum project, Project from Existing Source Code)</li><li class="atl-forced-newline"><strong>Business Process Modeling</strong> (BPMN 2.0 project)</li><li class="atl-forced-newline"><strong>Service-Oriented Modeling</strong> (Cameo SOA+ project)</li><li class="atl-forced-newline"><strong>Other</strong> (Project from Template, Process Guide project)</li></ul><p class="atl-forced-newline">Upon creation of a new project, the <strong>General-Purpose Modeling</strong> domain opens by default.</p><p class="atl-forced-newline"><br /></p><p class="atl-forced-newline">To create a new workspace for a blank project</p><hr /><ol><li class="atl-forced-newline">Do one of the following:</li></ol><ul><li style="list-style-type: none;background-image: none;"><ul><li class="atl-forced-newline">From the <strong>File</strong> menu, select <strong>New Project</strong>.</li><li class="atl-forced-newline">On the main toolbar, click the <strong>New Project</strong> button.</li><li class="atl-forced-newline">Press Ctrl + Shift + N.</li></ul></li></ul><p class="atl-forced-newline" style="margin-left: 60.0px;">Any of the above actions opens the <strong>New Project</strong> dialog. </p><p class="atl-forced-newline" style="margin-left: 30.0px;">2. Select the <strong>UML Project</strong> in the <strong>General-Purpose Modeling</strong> domain.</p><p class="atl-forced-newline" style="margin-left: 30.0px;">3. Specify the file name in the <strong>Name</strong> box.</p><p class="atl-forced-newline" style="margin-left: 30.0px;">4. Click the <ac:image ac:height="250"><ri:attachment ri:filename="new_project_dialog.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a new project" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> button to select the location to store the created project.</p><p class="atl-forced-newline" style="margin-left: 30.0px;">5. Click <strong>OK</strong>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2476cc15-2c7a-46a2-938a-95f5957aa355"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1428335648</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating projects" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Project Properties dialog" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Working with multiple projects" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

## PAGE 02625: (2026x) Creating a new slide for a presentation

- page_id: `272739756`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272739756/2026x+Creating+a+new+slide+for+a+presentation
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:51:51.955+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) General modeling features > (2026x) Report Wizard > (2026x) Template Creation Tutorial > (2026x) Report Wizard Directives > (2026x) #forpage directive
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

To create a new slide for a presentation

1. Open OpenOffice.org Impress or Microsoft PowerPoint.
2. Create two text boxes.
3. Type in: 
(i) one of the text boxes: text (ii) and type in the other text box: text The result will appear as shown in the figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

The result of the generated report will appear shown in the figure below. 
 
 [IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>To create a new slide for a presentation</p><hr /><ol><li>Open <a href="http://OpenOffice.org">OpenOffice.org</a> Impress or Microsoft PowerPoint.</li><li>Create two text boxes.</li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Type in:<br />(i) one of the text boxes:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="94c10f12-41cb-4d4d-a942-9a270ff64ad8"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#forpage($dia in $Diagram) 
$dia.name]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target">(ii) and type in the other text box:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a112c55c-f1ea-4b2b-9b37-d8c22d0489ae"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[$dia.image 
#endpage]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The result will appear as shown in the figure below.</p></li></ol><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddavbf2e5609b8cf026cfb71c63ca946bfca.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a new slide for a presentation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;">The result of the generated report will appear shown in the figure below.<br /> <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> <ac:image ac:title="A Generated Report" ac:alt="A Generated Report"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav8dfe469fffe291fde883d6b38b0573a7.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a new slide for a presentation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272733991 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02626: (2026x) Creating a relationship

- page_id: `272733991`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272733991/2026x+Creating+a+relationship
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:43:14.429+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) General modeling features > (2026x) Diagramming > (2026x) Working with paths and relationships
- labels: ['create-relationship', 'create-path', 'relationship-direction', 'relate-source-and-target', 'path-direction', 'path-target', 'msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

INLINE

You can create relationships between elements in one of the following ways:

- 
- 
- 

diagram_paneTo draw a path on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Understanding the user interface' space='']

1. In the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Understanding the user interface' space=''] , click the needed relationship.
2. Select the source shape of the path in the diagram pane.
3. Drop the path to the target shape. When drawing a new path, the optimal route is chosen automatically (the path avoids other shapes).When drawing a path between two elements, those shapes are bordered with a red or blue rectangle. The red highlight indicates that the path can not be drawn between these shapes. The blue highlight allows a path to be drawn. You can disable this highlighting in the **Environment Options** dialog under the Diagram node by setting the **Show Manipulation Highlighting** property value to *false*. For more information, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Customizing environment options' space=''].To draw a path faster, use the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Smart manipulator toolbar' space=''].

model_browserTo create relationships between selected elements

1. In the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Model Browser' space=''] , diagram pane, or table, right-click an element.
2. Choose Create Relation > Outgoing / Incoming and select the desired relationship type. The Create New <relation name> To / From dialog opens.
3. Do one or more of the following:
  - In the Model Browser, click the element to / from which you want to create a relationship.
  - On a diagram, click an element to / from which you want to create a relationship.
  - In the **Create New <relation name> To** /**From** dialog, click [IMAGE alt='' src='']. In the opened **Create New <relation name> To**/**From**dialog, choose the elementto / from which you want to create a relationship. If you do not know the exact location of the needed element, you can search for it in this step.
4. Click OK .

The **Active Selection** area shows the elements currently selected in the active diagram, table, matrix, map, or Model Browser. It changes with each click of the different elements when you switch to another diagram.

Tochoose one or several elements at a time,do one of the following

- Press Shift and click as many elements as you need.
- Click and drag the mouse pointer to select as many elements as you need.

The multiple selection works in one area at a time. For example, diagram pane, table, matrix, relation map, or Model Browser. If you need to select elements from several locations, you need to pin your current selection.

The **Pinned Selection** area shows the elements you choose to pin from the **Active Selection**. This is needed when you want to select elements from different locations (for example, you need to select elements from different diagrams, or from the diagram and Model Browser). In this case, you should pin each selection.

specificationTo create a relationship in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Specification window' space='']

1. Open the element's Specification window and select the Relations property group.
2. Click the Create Outgoing button or Create Incoming button and then select the desired relation type from the list.
3. In the Create New <relation name> To/ From dialog select an element to/ from which you want to create a relation and click OK . The relation appears in the Relations property group.

When the relationship is created, you can visually identify its direction (source and target elements) under the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Containment tab' space=''] >**Relations**.

The arrow between source and target elements appears if the path is created using only directed relationships.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

pane]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></span></span> <span class="confluence-link"><span style="color: rgb(51,102,255);"><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"> </span></span><span In the <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="d630b714-c2d6-41af-ae2c-fa10eefba204">opened <strong>Create New &lt;relation name&gt; To</strong><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> /</span><strong> From </strong>dialog</ac:inline-comment-marker>, choose the element<span> <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="c91d2740-784a-4746-bb8a-438080c3138f">to / from</ac:inline-comment-marker> which you want to create a relationship.<br /></span></span></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1ee4b3db-2c93-480a-99ff-2ebeec2038a4"><ac:rich-text-body><p>If you do not know the exact location of the needed element, you can search for it in this step.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ul></li><li>Click<strong> OK</strong>.</li></ol><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="create_new_relationship.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a relationship" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><p>The <strong>Active Selection</strong> area shows the elements currently <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="175a0936-7cef-4181-89da-4b1ef02a695b">selected <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">in the active diagram, table, matrix, map, or Model Browser</span></ac:inline-comment-marker>. It changes with each click of the different elements when you switch to another diagram.</p><p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="7c3f0f3b-09de-4337-8fa6-4e7e973667d7"><br /></ac:inline-comment-marker></p><p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="7c3f0f3b-09de-4337-8fa6-4e7e973667d7">To </ac:inline-comment-marker>choose one or several elements at a time<ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="7c3f0f3b-09de-4337-8fa6-4e7e973667d7">, </ac:inline-comment-marker><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="7c3f0f3b-09de-4337-8fa6-4e7e973667d7">do one of the following</ac:inline-comment-marker></span></p><hr /><ul><li>Press Shift and click as many elements as you need.</li><li>Click and drag the mouse pointer to select as many elements as you need.</li></ul><p>The multiple selection works in one area at a time. For example, diagram pane, table, matrix, relation map, or Model Browser. If you need to select elements from several locations, you need to pin your current selection.</p><p>The <strong>Pinned Selection</strong> area shows the elements you choose to pin from the <strong>Active Selection</strong>. <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="71f937c8-7f88-4df0-8c70-b71778086cae">This is needed when you want to select elements from different locations (for example, you need to select elements from different diagrams, or from the diagram and Model Browser)</ac:inline-comment-marker>. In this case, you should pin each selection<span>.</span></p><p><br /></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8c813c97-cf66-48c5-8c29-03fa98a9c2c6"><ac:parameter ac:name="">specification</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>To create a relationship in the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Specification window" /></ac:link></p><hr /><ol><li>Open the element's Specification window and select the <strong>Relations</strong> property group.</li><li>Click the <strong>Create Outgoing</strong> button or <strong>Create Incoming</strong> button and then select the desired relation type from the list.</li><li>In the <strong>Create New &lt;relation name&gt; To/ From</strong> dialog select an element to/ from which you want to create a relation and click <strong>OK</strong>.<br />The relation appears in the <strong>Relations</strong> property group.</li></ol><p>When the relationship is created, you can visually identify its direction (source and target elements) under the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Containment tab" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Containment tree]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> &gt;<strong> Relations</strong>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="223ba6d2-d116-41cb-b0f8-2c839ac04069"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The arrow between source and target elements appears if the path is created using only directed relationships.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><ac:image ac:title="Identifying the relationship's direction" ac:alt="Identifying the relationship's direction"><ri:attachment ri:filename="relation_direction_arrows_under_containment_tree_relations.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a relationship" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272739708 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02627: (2026x) Creating a report including the Actor, Use Cases, and diagram image names

- page_id: `272739708`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272739708/2026x+Creating+a+report+including+the+Actor+Use+Cases+and+diagram+image+names
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:51:45.415+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) General modeling features > (2026x) Report Wizard > (2026x) Template Creation Tutorial > (2026x) Linking report templates to the modeling tool > (2026x) How modeling tool adds its elements to velocity context
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

135588550

135588551

135588549

**On this page**

3

135588551

##### Creating a report including the names of actors, use cases, and diagram images

This section will use part of the Inventory Control System.mdzip file as an example to illustrate how to reference elements in a report. This example contains several packages. For instance, if you want to use the 'Use Case View' package, you need to know the elements inside the package before using it. To do this, follow the instructions in the previous section, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating Your First Template' space=''].

The 'Use Case View' package contains three element types: Actor, UseCase, and Diagram that you can name in a template by typing **"$Actor**," **"$UseCase**," and **"$Diagram**" respectively. The following example shows you how to create a report that will print the names of the Actors, UseCases, and diagram images.

To create a report that includes the names of Actor, Use Cases, and diagram images

1. Open Microsoft Word or OpenOffice.org Writer.
2. Use the **#foreach** directive to iterate through **$Actor**, **$UseCase**, and **$Diagram**. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] Using #foreach directive to iterate through $Actor, $UseCase, and $Diagram in a Rich Text Format template.
3. Use Report Wizard to add the template to the modeling tool.
4. With the " Inventory Control System.mdzip " project open, open the Report Wizard dialog and select your template.
5. Select the element scope so it covers only the "Use Case View" package. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] Selecting the Use Case View package from the model as the element scope to be included in the report.
6. Complete all steps in the **Report Wizard** dialog, and generate the report. The output will be shown as in the following figure. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] Shows a partial report output. A full report will print all the names of the Actors, Use Cases, and the Use Case diagrams.

##### Glossary

- Actor An actor models a type of role played by an entity that interacts with the subjects of its associated use cases, e.g., by exchanging signal and data. Actors may represent roles played by human users, external hardware, or other systems.
- Element T he abstract root UML metaclass. It has no superclass in the hierarchy of UML elements. It is the superclass for all metaclasses in the UML infrastructure library. There is no general notation for an element. The specific subclasses of an element define their own notation. Each UMLelementhas acompositionrelationship to itself to support the capability for elements toownother elements.
- Use case A specification of behavior. A use case is a kind of BehavioredClassifier that represents a declaration of a set of offered Behaviors. Each use case specifies some behavior that a subject can perform in collaboration with one or more actors. A UseCase may be owned either by a Package or by a Classifier. Use cases are a means to capture the requirements of systems,( i.e. what systems are supposed to do).

##### Sample model

[ATTACHMENT filename='Inventory Control System.mdzip']250

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Exercise: Building a simple class diagram report' space='']

## PAGE 02629: (2026x) Creating a SysML Internal Block diagram for a Block

- page_id: `272731107`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272731107/2026x+Creating+a+SysML+Internal+Block+diagram+for+a+Block
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:38:18.341+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) SysML v1 modeling > (2026x) Modeling structure with Blocks > (2026x) Decomposing Blocks
- labels: ['mcse', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

To create a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) SysML Internal Block Diagram' space=''] for a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Block' space='MED']

1. Select the Block shape on the diagram pane. The smart manipulator menu appears.
2. Click the SysML Internal Block diagram button [ATTACHMENT filename='ibd_icon.png'] . The SysML Internal Block diagram is created and owned by the selected Block. The SysML Internal Block diagram and the owner Block have the same name. The [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Defining hyperlinks1' space=''] to the created diagram is added to the Block.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>To create a <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) SysML Internal Block Diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[SysML Internal Block diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> for a <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Block" /></ac:link></p><hr /><ol><li>Select the Block shape on the diagram pane. The smart manipulator menu appears.</li><li>Click the SysML Internal Block diagram button <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:alt="SysML Internal Block Diagram" ac:width="20"><ri:attachment ri:filename="ibd_icon.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a SysML Internal Block diagram for a Block" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>.<br />The SysML Internal Block diagram is created and owned by the selected Block. The SysML Internal Block diagram and the owner Block have the same name. The <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Defining hyperlinks1" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[hyperlink ]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>to the created diagram is added to the Block.</li></ol>
````

## PAGE 02630: (2026x) Creating a template for a Rich Text Format or OpenDocument Text document, or Microsoft Word Document (DOCX)

- page_id: `272739736`
- space_key: `MT`

3

1358508564

#### Creating a template for a Rich Text Format or OpenDocument Text document, or Microsoft Word Document (DOCX)

You can create a document in .rtf, .odt, or .docx file format and add it to the modeling tool as a report template.

To create a template for a Rich Text Format or OpenDocument Text document, or Microsoft Word Document (*DOCX*)

1. Open OpenOffice.org Writer or Microsoft Word for an *.odt , *.rtf , or *.docx file. (To save a file as an *.odt file in Microsoft Word, an add-on is required. You can get the add-on at [http://odf-converter.sourceforge.net/](http://odf-converter.sourceforge.net/) .)
2. Add the Velocity Template Language codes to the template file.
3. Save the template file.
4. Add the template to the modeling tool.

When generating a document, Report Wizard will apply the text format used in writing the Velocity code.

For instance, when typing:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

the generated output will be:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### Glossary

- Velocity A Java-based template engine. It permits anyone to use a simple, yet powerful, template language to reference objects defined in Java code . Velocity is a project of the[Apache Software Foundation](http://www.apache.org/), charged with the creation and maintenance of open-source software related to the[Apache Velocity Engine](http://velocity.apache.org/engine/index.html). All software created at the Velocity project is available under the[Apache Software License](http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0.txt)and free of charge for the public.

#### More information about working with report templates in Report Wizard

To learn more about working with report templates, visit the following pages:

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating a template for an HTML, a text, an XML, or a DocBook document' space='']

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating a template for an OpenDocument Presentation (ODP) or Microsoft PowerPoint presentation (PPTX)' space='']

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating a template for an OpenDocument spreadsheet or Microsoft Excel spreadsheet' space='']

1358508560

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

(To save a file as an <em>*.odt</em> file in Microsoft Word, an add-on is required. You can get the add-on at <em><a class="external-link" href="http://odf-converter.sourceforge.net/" rel="nofollow">http://odf-converter.sourceforge.net/</a></em>.)</li><li>Add the Velocity Template Language codes to the template file.</li><li>Save the template file.</li><li>Add the template to the modeling tool.</li></ol><p>When generating a document, Report Wizard will apply the text format used in writing the Velocity code.</p><p>For instance, when typing:</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="HelloWorld_1.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a template for a Rich Text Format or OpenDocument Text document, or Microsoft Word Document (DOCX)" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p> the generated output will be:</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="HelloWorld_2.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a template for a Rich Text Format or OpenDocument Text document, or Microsoft Word Document (DOCX)" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h2>Glossary</h2><ul><li><strong>Velocity</strong><span style="color: rgb(106,106,106);"><br />A</span><span style="color: rgb(84,84,84);"> Java-based template engine. It permits anyone to use a simple, yet powerful, template language to reference objects defined in Java </span><span style="color: rgb(106,106,106);">code</span><span style="color: rgb(84,84,84);">. <span>Velocity is a project of the </span><a href="http://www.apache.org/">Apache Software Foundation</a><span>, charged with the creation and maintenance of open-source software related to the </span><a href="http://velocity.apache.org/engine/index.html">Apache Velocity Engine</a><span>. All software created at the Velocity project is available under the </span><a href="http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0.txt">Apache Software License</a><span> and free of charge for the public.</span></span></li></ul><h2>More information about working with report templates in Report Wizard</h2><p>To learn more about working with report templates, visit the following pages:</p><p><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a template for an HTML, a text, an XML, or a DocBook document" /></ac:link></p><p><ac:link /></p><p><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a template for an OpenDocument Presentation (ODP) or Microsoft PowerPoint presentation (PPTX)" /></ac:link></p><p><span style="color: rgb(84,84,84);"><span><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a template for an OpenDocument spreadsheet or Microsoft Excel spreadsheet" /></ac:link><br /></span></span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8f8689b9-d7aa-4ec9-be33-943d3fd4a880"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1358508560</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body /></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272739706 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02631: (2026x) Creating a template for an HTML, a text, an XML, or a DocBook document

- page_id: `272739706`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272739706/2026x+Creating+a+template+for+an+HTML+a+text+an+XML+or+a+DocBook+document
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:51:44.998+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) General modeling features > (2026x) Report Wizard > (2026x) Template Creation Tutorial > (2026x) Linking report templates to the modeling tool > (2026x) Supported file types
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

To create a template for an *HTML*, a *text*, an *XML*, or a *DocBook* document

1. Open a text editor.
2. Add the code to the text file.
3. Save the template file (with a correct filename extension).
4. Add the template to Report Wizard.

- When you generate a report from a particular template, Report Wizard will automatically create an HTML , text , an XML , or a DocBook document depending on a given template filename extension.
- The #forrow , #forcol , and #forpage directives do not support HTML , text, XML , and DocBook format files.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>To create a template for an <em>HTML</em>, a <em>text</em>, an <em>XML</em>, or a <em>DocBook</em> document</p><hr /><ol><li>Open a text editor.</li><li>Add the code to the text file.</li><li>Save the template file (with a correct filename extension).</li><li>Add the template to Report Wizard.<br /> </li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6853e0b9-8381-4096-9c74-cccd903d3c91"><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>When you generate a report from a particular template, Report Wizard will automatically create an <em>HTML</em>, <em>text</em>, an <em>XML</em>, or a <em>DocBook</em> document depending on a given template filename extension.</li><li>The <strong>#forrow</strong>,<strong> #forcol</strong>, and <strong>#forpage</strong> directives do not support <em>HTML</em>, <em>text, XML</em>, and <em>DocBook</em> format files.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272739718 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02632: (2026x) Creating a template for an OpenDocument Presentation (ODP) or Microsoft PowerPoint presentation (PPTX)

- page_id: `272739718`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272739718/2026x+Creating+a+template+for+an+OpenDocument+Presentation+ODP+or+Microsoft+PowerPoint+presentation+PPTX
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:51:46.080+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) General modeling features > (2026x) Report Wizard > (2026x) Template Creation Tutorial > (2026x) Linking report templates to the modeling tool > (2026x) Supported file types
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1351510228

1351510229

1351510227

**On this page**

3

1351510229

To create a template for an OpenDocument Presentation (ODP) or Microsoft PowerPoint presentation (*PPTX*)

1. Open OpenOffice.org Impress or Microsoft PowerPoint.
2. Add the code to the template file.
3. Save the template file.
4. Add the template to Report Wizard.

A presentation document is a special document template that does not contain a content order. It uses a text box to insert text as the content of the document. A text box is an image structure (an image structure keeps the position of each image in x, y coordinates). You can change the position of a text box, or place it in the same position as others.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Writing VTL in a text box in [OpenOffice.org](http://OpenOffice.org) Impress.

The following are the rules that you need to follow when using the **#if,****#elseif**, **#else**, **#foreach**, **#forpage**, and **#macro** directives in **.odp* and *.pptx* documents:

- Statement VTL statements that maintain a sequence order, such as **#if** and **#foreach**, must begin and end the statements inside the same text box. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] Valid use of multi-line statement in ODP. Do not split the statements into several text boxes, as this will cause an error. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] Invalid use of a multi-line statement in ODP.
- #macro statement A #macro statement must be declared in the same text box. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] Valid use of macro statement in ODP.
- #forpage statement The **#forpage** statement is different from the others, as it does not contain any order. It can begin and end the statement in different text boxes. Its value can also be used in different text boxes. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] Valid use of the #forpage statement in ODP. To use a structured statement such as **#if**, **#macro**, or **#foreach** with the **#forpage** statement, ensure that the statement is in the same text box. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] Valid use of #if and #foreach with #forpage statement. Using an example from the previous figure, the generated result will be as follows: [IMAGE alt='' src=''] The end result of using #if and #foreach with #forpage statement.

#### Glossary

- VTL TheVelocity Template Language(VTL) is meant to provide an easy, simple, and clean way to incorporate dynamic content in a web page. A VTL statementbegins with the # character and contains a directive: set .

#### More information about working with Velocity Template Language (VTL)

To learn more about working with Velocity Template Language (VTL), visit the following pages:

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Velocity templating language' space='']

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Velocity variable' space='']

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Velocity Directives' space='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272739728 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02633: (2026x) Creating a template for an OpenDocument spreadsheet or Microsoft Excel spreadsheet

- page_id: `272739728`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272739728/2026x+Creating+a+template+for+an+OpenDocument+spreadsheet+or+Microsoft+Excel+spreadsheet
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:51:46.847+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) General modeling features > (2026x) Report Wizard > (2026x) Template Creation Tutorial > (2026x) Linking report templates to the modeling tool > (2026x) Supported file types
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

To create a template for an OpenDocument spreadsheet or Microsoft Excel spreadsheet

1. Open OpenOffice.org Calc or Microsoft Excel.
2. Add the code to the template file.
3. Save the template file.
4. Add the template to Report Wizard.

All multiline directives such as **#if, #else,****#elseif**, **#foreach**, and **#macro** must be used under the following conditions:

1. The beginning and end of a statement must be declared within a single cell. A #macro statement must be declared in a single cell. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav195c3297d20439835819f6152c9fb7fe.png']
2. You can use #foreach in a single cell record only. To create data for multiple rows, use the #forrow directive instead, see figure below. [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav6f99f5e91aea399e24eac62e0953d947.png']
3. You can use #forcol to create data for multiple columns, see figure below. You can use the statement in conjunction with #forrow . [ATTACHMENT filename='worddav55c194a898ab2d279c85ddb229cdf770.png']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>To create a template for an OpenDocument spreadsheet or Microsoft Excel spreadsheet</p><hr /><ol><li>Open OpenOffice.org Calc or Microsoft Excel.</li><li>Add the code to the template file.</li><li>Save the template file.</li><li>Add the template to Report Wizard.</li></ol><p>All multiline directives such as <strong>#if, #else, </strong><strong>#elseif</strong>, <strong>#foreach</strong>, and <strong>#macro</strong> must be used under the following conditions:</p><ol><li>The beginning and end of a statement must be declared within a single cell. A <strong>#macro</strong> statement must be declared in a single cell.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav195c3297d20439835819f6152c9fb7fe.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a template for an OpenDocument spreadsheet or Microsoft Excel spreadsheet" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>You can use <strong>#foreach</strong> in a single cell record only. To create data for multiple rows, use the <strong>#forrow</strong> directive instead, see figure below.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav6f99f5e91aea399e24eac62e0953d947.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a template for an OpenDocument spreadsheet or Microsoft Excel spreadsheet" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>You can use <strong>#forcol</strong> to create data for multiple columns, see figure below. You can use the statement in conjunction with <strong>#forrow</strong>.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav55c194a898ab2d279c85ddb229cdf770.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a template for an OpenDocument spreadsheet or Microsoft Excel spreadsheet" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272739888 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02634: (2026x) Creating a UAF project

- page_id: `272739888`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272739888/2026x+Creating+a+UAF+project
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:52:10.765+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) UAF 1.3 modeling
- labels: ['new-project', 'create-blank-uaf-project', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A project is a physical working unit that consists of model, model visualizations(diagrams, tables, matrices, etc.), configuration data and referencesto other elements residing in used projects.

To create a project

1. Do one of the following:
  - Select **File**> **New** **Project**.
  - On the main toolbar, click the **New** **Project** button.
  - Press Ctrl+Shift+N.
2. In the **New****Project** dialog, under **Enterprise Modeling**, select the****project type you want to create.
3. Specify name, location, and other preferences if needed.
4. Click **OK** when you are done.

By default, **UAF Project** will be selected in the **General** property group. You can select any of the available Architectural Frameworks.

You can always change the Architectural Framework in the **Project Options** dialog **General project options** group.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

When creating a new project, in the New Project dialog, you can choose which Framework views will be created and how will they be ordered.

You can select any of the available Architectural Frameworks.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="998fe568-6567-43b5-9d11-9a5ccd847db7"><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can always change the Architectural Framework in the <strong>Project Options</strong> dialog <strong>General project options</strong> group.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="new_project.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a UAF project" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>When creating a new project, in the New Project dialog, you can choose which Framework views will be created and how will they be ordered.<br /><span style="color:var(--ds-chart-gray-bold,#8590a2);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-chart-gray-bold,#8590a2);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-chart-gray-bold,#8590a2);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-chart-gray-bold,#8590a2);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-chart-gray-bold,#8590a2);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-chart-gray-bold,#8590a2);"> </span></p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272736095 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02635: (2026x) Creating a validation dashboard

- page_id: `272736095`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272736095/2026x+Creating+a+validation+dashboard
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:46:38.800+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) General modeling features > (2026x) Tools > (2026x) Metric Suites > (2026x) Building validation-based metric definitions
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

If you have several Metric tables where you calculate metrics using Metric Suites with validation-based metric definitions, you can display the validation results from these tables in a validation dashboard.

800450

To create a validation dashboard follow these steps:

1. .
2. .

##### Creating Metric Suites and Metric Tables to track in a validation dashboard

You can calculate model validation results in Metric Tables based on Metric Suites with validation-based metric definitions. Metrics calculated in these Metric Tables can later be displayed in a validation dashboard to track all validation results in one place. Follow the steps below to create Metric Suites and Metric Tables for a validation dashboard.

To create Metric Suites for a validation dashboard

1. Create a Package to store your Metric Suites.
2. Create a Profile Diagram and name it (e.g., *Metric Suites*).
3. In the Profile diagram, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Adding a new Metric Suite' space='']Create a Metric Suite using the *BaseMetricSuite* for each validation rule you want to track.
4. Create [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Building validation-based metric definitions' space=''] for the Metric Suites created in the previous step. Example of a Metric Suite that tracks a validation ruleFor example, if you want to track the Unused Constraint Parameter validation rule, you can create a Metric Suite named *Unused Constrained Parameter* with the following validation-based metric definitions:Validation-based metric definition named*****Constrained Parameter* with the **Type** property set to **Integer**, **Validation Rule** property set to **Unused Constrained Parameter**, and the **Metric Type** property set to **Element Quantity**.Validation-based metric definition named *Unused Constrained Parameter* with the **Type** property set to **Integer**, **Validation Rule** property set to **Unused Constrained Parameter**, and the **Metric Type** property set to **Failed Element Quantity**.Validation-based metric definition named *Unused Constrained Parameter Percentage* with the **Type** property set to **Real**, **Validation Rule** property set to **Unused Constrained Parameter**, and the **Metric Type** property set to **Failed Element Percentage**.
5. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating a Metric Table' space=''] for each Metric Suite created in step 3 and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Calculating metrics in the Metric Table' space=''] .

##### Creating a validation dashboard

A validation dashboard is a convenient way to track model validation results in one place. It displays metric values from multiple Metric Tables created from validation-based Metric Suites.

To create a validation dashboard

1. Open the Profile Diagram in which you created Metric suites with validation based metric definitions (see step 2 in ).
2. In the Profile diagram, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Adding a new Metric Suite' space='']Create a Metric Suite using the *BaseMetricSuite* and name it (e.g., Validation Dashboard ). [ATTACHMENT filename='creating_validation_dashboard_metric_suite.png']
3. Open the Specification window of the newly created Metric Suite (e.g., Validation Dashboard Metric Suite, as displayed above) and set the Is Abstract property to true .
4. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Building validation-based metric definitions' space=''] for the Metric Suite to define the structure of the dashboard. Each validation-based metric definition represents a column in the validation dashboard. Example of validation-based metric definitionsFor example, if you want to create a validation dashboard with the *Elements*, *Failed Element Quantity*, and *Failed Element Percentage* columns, create the following validation-based metric definitions:Validation-based metric definition named*****Elements* with the **Type** property set to **Integer** and the **Metric Type** property set to **Element Quantity**.Validation-based metric definition named *Failed Element Quantity* with the **Type** property set to **Integer** and the **Metric Type** property set to **Failed Element Quantity**.Validation-based metric definition named *Failed Element Percentage* with the **Type** property set to **Real** and the **Metric Type** property set to **Failed Element Percentage**.
5. In the same Profile diagram, create a Generalization relationship from each Metric Suite that you created to track a validation rule (step 3 in ) to the abstract Metric Suite created in step 2. [ATTACHMENT filename='creating_metric_suites_to_track_validation_rules.png']
6. Link each validation-based metric definition that calculates the metric you want to display in the validation dashboard to the metric definition representing a specific validation dashboard column (see step 4), as described below:
  1. Open the Specification window of the validation-based metric definition that calculates the metric you want to display in the validation dashboard.
  2. Select the specification cell of the Redefined Property property and click [ATTACHMENT filename='3dot_short.png'] .
  3. On the left side of the Select Property window, find the abstract Metric Suite created in step 2 and select its metric definition representing the validation dashboard column in which you want to display the metric.
  4. Click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] to add the metric definition to the **Selected elements** area. Click **OK**. Example of linking a validation-based metric definitionLet's say you have the *Unused Constraint Parameter* Metric Suite with the *Constraint Parameter* validation-based metric definition that calculates the number of constraint parameters. If you want to display the number of constraint parametersin the *Elements* column of the validation dashboard, you must open the Specification window of the the*Constraint Parameter*validation-based metric definition, and select the *Elements* validation-based metric definition of the abstract Metric Suite (created in step 2) as the value of the **Redefined Property** property.
  5. Repeat steps a through d for each validation-based metric definition calculating the metric you want to display in the validation dashboard.
7. Build the validation dashboard:
  1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating a Metric Table' space=''] that will be your validation dashboard.
  2. In the Metric table, click [ATTACHMENT filename='3dot_long.png'] next to the Metric Suite box.
  3. In the Select Metric Suite dialog, select the abstract Metric Suite created in step 2. Click OK .
  4. Open the Specification window of the Metric table.
  5. Select the specification cell of the Scope property and click [ATTACHMENT filename='3dot_short.png'] .
  6. On the left side of the **Select Elements** dialog, select the Metric Tables you want to track in the validation dashboard. Selecting multiple elementsIf you cannot select multiple elements, click the **Multiple Selection** button on the bottom of the **Select Elements** dialog.
  7. Click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] to add the selected Metric tables to the **Selected elements** area. Click **OK**.
  8. In the Specification widow of the Metric Table, select the specification cell of the Query property and click [ATTACHMENT filename='3dot_short.png'] .
  9. On the left side of the **Query** dialog, select **Create operation** and click the **Operation from Model** operation on the right side of the dialog. Enabling the Expert modeThe **Operation from Model** operation is only available in the Expert mode. To enable the Expert mode, click the **Expert** button on the bottom left corner of the **Query** dialog.
  10. In the **Select Opaque Behavior** dialog, select the *LatestMetricInstances* Opaque Behavior. Click **OK**.
  11. On the left side of the Query dialog, select Element = unspecified located under the LatestMetricInstances1 operation and click the Reset button. [ATTACHMENT filename='removing_argument_of_parameter.png']
  12. On the right side of the Query dialog, select the Contextual Variable operation in the Other operation group and click OK .
  13. Close the Specification window of the Metric Table.

The latest instances from the specified Metric Tables are now collected and displayed in your validation dashboard, as shown below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### This sample validation dashboard tracks the latest metrics from three Metric Tables.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>If you have several Metric tables where you calculate metrics using Metric Suites with validation-based metric definitions, you can display the validation results from these tables in a validation dashboard.</p><p><br /></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:structured-macro ac:name="widget" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d6843c2c-827d-4099-ab8c-34bafd181fb8"><ac:parameter ac:name="width">800</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="url"><ri:url ri:value="http://youtube.com/watch?v=5QJnTyUXvF0" /></ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="height">450</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p><p style="text-align: center;"><br /></p><p>To create a validation dashboard follow these steps:</p><ol><li><ac:link ac:anchor="Creating Metric Suites and Metric Tables for a validation dashboard"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Create Metric Suites for validation rules you want to track and create Metric Tables to calculate validation results]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li><li><ac:link ac:anchor="Creating a validation dashboard"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Create a validation dashboard that displays metric values from the Metric Tables created in the previous step]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li></ol><h3><br />Creating Metric Suites and Metric Tables to track in a validation dashboard</h3><p>You can calculate model validation results in Metric Tables based on Metric Suites with validation-based metric definitions. Metrics calculated in these Metric Tables can later be displayed in a validation dashboard to track all validation results in one place. Follow the steps below to create Metric Suites and Metric Tables for a validation dashboard.</p><p><br /></p><p>To create Metric Suites for a validation dashboard</p><hr /><ol><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Create a Package to store your Metric Suites.</p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Create a Profile Diagram and name it (e.g., <em>Metric Suites</em>).</p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="0e0a2fe7-5c38-4e6c-95b1-ea291c49f2e1">In the Profile diagram</ac:inline-comment-marker>, <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Adding a new Metric Suite" /><ac:link-body>Create a Metric Suite using the <em>BaseMetricSuite</em></ac:link-body></ac:link> for each validation rule you want to track.</p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Create <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Building validation-based metric definitions" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[validation-based metric definitions]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> for the Metric Suites created in the previous step.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9b0541b0-3db5-4461-978b-dab84afeab2b"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Example of a Metric Suite that tracks a validation rule</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>For example, if you want to track the Unused Constraint Parameter validation rule, you can create a Metric Suite named <em>Unused Constrained Parameter</em> with the following validation-based metric definitions:</p><ul><li>Validation-based metric definition named<strong> </strong><em>Constrained Parameter</em> with the <strong>Type</strong> property set to <strong>Integer</strong>, <strong>Validation Rule</strong> property set to <strong>Unused Constrained Parameter</strong>, and the <strong>Metric Type</strong> property set to <strong>Element Quantity</strong>.</li><li>Validation-based metric definition named <em>Unused Constrained Parameter</em> with the <strong>Type</strong> property set to <strong>Integer</strong>, <strong>Validation Rule</strong> property set to <strong>Unused Constrained Parameter</strong>, and the <strong>Metric Type</strong> property set to <strong>Failed Element Quantity</strong>.</li><li>Validation-based metric definition named <em>Unused Constrained Parameter Percentage</em> with the <strong>Type</strong> property set to <strong>Real</strong>, <strong>Validation Rule</strong> property set to <strong>Unused Constrained Parameter</strong>, and the <strong>Metric Type</strong> property set to <strong>Failed Element Percentage</strong>.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a Metric Table" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Create a Metric Table]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> for each Metric Suite created in step 3 and <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Calculating metrics in the Metric Table" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[calculate metrics]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li></ol><h3><br />Creating a validation dashboard</h3><p>A validation dashboard is a convenient way to track model validation results in one place. It displays metric values from multiple Metric Tables created from validation-based Metric Suites.</p><p><br /></p><p>To create a validation dashboard</p><hr /><ol><li>Open the Profile Diagram in which you created Metric suites with validation based metric definitions (see step 2 in <ac:link ac:anchor="Creating Metric Suites and Metric Tables for a validation dashboard" />).</li><li>In the Profile diagram, <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Adding a new Metric Suite" /><ac:link-body>Create a Metric Suite using the <em>BaseMetricSuite</em></ac:link-body></ac:link> and name it (e.g., <em>Validation Dashboard</em>).<br /><br /><ac:image ac:height="204"><ri:attachment ri:filename="creating_validation_dashboard_metric_suite.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a validation dashboard" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Open the Specification window of the newly created Metric Suite (e.g., <em>Validation Dashboard</em> Metric Suite, as displayed above) and set the <strong>Is Abstract</strong> property to <em>true</em>.</li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Building validation-based metric definitions" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Build validation-based metric definitions]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> for the Metric Suite to define the structure of the dashboard. Each validation-based metric definition represents a column in the validation dashboard.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="880adc39-9e49-4229-a38f-2f77f7d8bb9f"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Example of validation-based metric definitions</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>For example, if you want to create a validation dashboard with the <em><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="a23eecf2-6c61-4402-9008-ba8633f4bc6f">Elements</ac:inline-comment-marker></em>, <em>Failed Element Quantity</em>, and <em>Failed Element Percentage</em> columns, create the following validation-based metric definitions:</p><ul><li>Validation-based metric definition named<strong> </strong><em>Elements</em> with the <strong>Type</strong> property set to <strong>Integer</strong> and the <strong>Metric Type</strong> property set to <strong>Element Quantity</strong>.</li><li>Validation-based metric definition named <em>Failed Element Quantity</em> with the <strong>Type</strong> property set to <strong>Integer</strong> and the <strong>Metric Type</strong> property set to <strong>Failed Element Quantity</strong>.</li><li>Validation-based metric definition named <em>Failed Element Percentage</em> with the <strong>Type</strong> property set to <strong>Real</strong> and the <strong>Metric Type</strong> property set to <strong>Failed Element Percentage</strong>.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li>In the same Profile diagram, create a Generalization relationship from each Metric Suite that you created to track a validation rule (step 3 in <ac:link ac:anchor="Creating Metric Suites and Metric Tables to track in a validation dashboard" />) to the abstract Metric Suite created in step 2.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="creating_metric_suites_to_track_validation_rules.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a validation dashboard" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Link each validation-based metric definition that calculates the metric you want to display in the validation dashboard to the metric definition representing a specific validation dashboard column (see step 4), as described below:<ol><li>Open the Specification window of the validation-based metric definition that calculates the metric you want to display in the validation dashboard.</li><li>Select the specification cell of the <strong>Redefined Property</strong> property and click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="15"><ri:attachment ri:filename="3dot_short.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="_buttons" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>.</li><li>On the left side of the <strong>Select Property</strong> window, find the abstract Metric Suite created in step 2 and select its metric definition representing the validation dashboard column in which you want to display the metric.</li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="23"><ri:attachment ri:filename="add_button_long.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="_buttons" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> to add the metric definition to the <strong>Selected elements</strong> area. Click <strong>OK</strong>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6b84c8b8-b4cb-4528-84ab-0ad56e89d188"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Example of linking a validation-based metric definition</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Let's say you have the <em>Unused Constraint Parameter</em> Metric Suite with the <em>Constraint Parameter</em> validation-based metric definition that calculates the number of constraint parameters. If you want to display the number of constraint parameters<span> in the <em>Elements</em> column of the validation dashboard, you must open the Specification window of the <span>the </span><em>Constraint Parameter</em><span> validation-based metric definition, and</span> select the <em>Elements</em> validation-based metric definition of the abstract Metric Suite (created in step 2) as the value of the <strong>Redefined Property</strong> property.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li>Repeat steps a through d for each validation-based metric definition calculating the metric you want to display in the validation dashboard.</li></ol></li><li>Build the validation dashboard:<ol><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a Metric Table" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Create a Metric Table]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> that will be your validation dashboard.</li><li>In the Metric table, click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="16"><ri:attachment ri:filename="3dot_long.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="_buttons" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> next to the <strong>Metric Suite</strong> box.</li><li>In the <strong>Select Metric Suite</strong> dialog, select the abstract Metric Suite created in step 2. Click <strong>OK</strong>.</li><li>Open the Specification window of the Metric table.</li><li>Select the specification cell of the <strong>Scope</strong> property and click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="15"><ri:attachment ri:filename="3dot_short.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="_buttons" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>.</li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">On the left side of the <strong>Select Elements</strong> dialog, select the Metric Tables you want to track in the validation dashboard.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="50a2baa7-ce3c-4612-b681-f33ede79ebcf"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Selecting multiple elements</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>If you cannot select multiple elements, click the <strong>Multiple Selection</strong> button on the bottom of the <strong>Select Elements</strong> dialog.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="23"><ri:attachment ri:filename="add_button_long.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="_buttons" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> to add the selected Metric tables to the <strong>Selected elements</strong> area. Click <strong>OK</strong>.</p></li><li>In the Specification widow of the Metric Table, select the specification cell of the <strong>Query</strong> property and click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="15"><ri:attachment ri:filename="3dot_short.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="_buttons" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>.</li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">On the left side of the <strong>Query</strong> dialog, select <strong>Create operation</strong> and click the <strong>Operation from Model</strong> operation on the right side of the dialog.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="af9ac358-c815-40b2-9303-be41418596b0"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Enabling the Expert mode</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>The <strong>Operation from Model</strong> operation is only available in the Expert mode. To enable the Expert mode, click the <strong>Expert</strong> button on the bottom left corner of the <strong>Query</strong> dialog.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">In the <strong>Select Opaque Behavior</strong> dialog, select the <em>LatestMetricInstances</em> Opaque Behavior. Click <strong>OK</strong>.</p></li><li>On the left side of the <strong>Query</strong> dialog, select <strong>Element = unspecified</strong> located under the <em>LatestMetricInstances1</em> operation and click the <strong>Reset</strong> button.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="removing_argument_of_parameter.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a validation dashboard" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>On the right side of the <strong>Query</strong> dialog, select the <strong>Contextual Variable</strong> operation in the <strong>Other</strong> operation group and click <strong>OK</strong>.</li><li>Close the Specification window of the Metric Table.</li></ol></li></ol><p><br />The latest instances from the specified Metric Tables are now collected and displayed in your validation dashboard, as shown below.<br /><br /></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="sample_validation_dashboard.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating a validation dashboard" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">This sample validation dashboard tracks the latest metrics from three Metric Tables.</h6>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272734151 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02636: (2026x) Creating A8 Standards Forecast table

- page_id: `272734151`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272734151/2026x+Creating+A8+Standards+Forecast+table
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:43:23.099+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) UAF 1.3 modeling > (2026x) NAF 4.0 > (2026x) Architecture Meta-Data viewpoint > (2026x) A8 Standards
- labels: ['create-a8-standards-forecast-table', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1658841395

1658841406

1658841396

To create an A8 Standards Forecast table

1. Do one of the following:
  - Press Ctrl+N.
  - From the main menu, select Diagrams > Create Diagram .
  - Right-click the <view name> Package and select Create Diagram > <diagram name> .
  - In the main toolbar, click Create Diagram and in the opened window, search for the needed diagram. The [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Resources Forecast Creation Wizard' space=''] opens.
2. Follow the steps of the wizard.

When the table is created, you can later add new or existing resources as well as add or remove columns.

##### Adding rows

To add rows with new resources

1. In the Resources Forecast table toolbar, click Add New .
2. Choose a resource type from the list.
3. In the opened Select Owner dialog, choose the owner.
4. In the Specification dialog, type the name for the new resource.

To add rows with existing resources

1. In the Resources Forecast table toolbar, click Add Existing .
2. In the opened Select Element dialog, choose a needed resource.
3. Click OK .

##### Adding new columns

To add new columns

1. In the Resources Forecast table toolbar, click Add / Remove Columns .
2. In the opened Select Actual Strategic Phase dialog, choose one or more needed Actual Strategic Phases.
3. Click OK .

##### Filling cells

When you add rows and columns, you can begin filling out the cells by adding Subjects of Forecast.

If you add the resources which are connected through the Forecast relationship, the Subject of Forecast is added automatically after creating a table.

To add a Subject of Forecast

1. Double-click a needed cell.
2. Click the ... button.
3. In the **Select Subject of Forecast** dialog, choose the resources you need to add. You can also create resources using the Creation Mode.
4. Click OK , when you are done.

When you add the resources into cells, the Forecast relationship is created between the added resource and the resource which represents the row. The relationship's Start and End dates are filled according to the dates defined in the relevant Actual Strategic Phase.

##### Removing columns

To remove columns

1. In the Resources Forecast table toolbar, click Add / Remove Columns .
2. In the opened Select Actual Strategic Phase dialog, remove Actual Strategic Phases from selection or click Remove All. [ATTACHMENT filename='remove_columns.png']
3. Click OK .

##### Sorting columns

After you add new columns, you can sort them according to Actual Strategic Phase dates.

To sort columns

- In the Resources Forecast table toolbar, click Sort Columns .

1658841393

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Resources Forecast Creation Wizard' space='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272734154 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02637: (2026x) Creating A8 Standards table

- page_id: `272734154`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272734154/2026x+Creating+A8+Standards+table
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:43:23.886+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) UAF 1.3 modeling > (2026x) NAF 4.0 > (2026x) Architecture Meta-Data viewpoint > (2026x) A8 Standards
- labels: ['create-a8-standards-table', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

To create an A8 Standards table, do either

- Add a new element by clicking the Add New button and select element. The choose the element owner in the Select Owner dialog.
- Add the existing elements by clicking the Add Existing button and in the Select UAF element dialog select the elements.
- Assign one or more Standards or Protocols to each row that the particular UAF element must conform to by clicking the **…** button on any cell in the **Standard/Policy** column.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>To create an A8 Standards table, do either</p><hr /><ul><li>Add a new element by clicking the <strong>Add New</strong> button and select element. The choose the element owner in the <strong>Select Owner</strong> dialog.</li><li>Add the existing elements by clicking the<strong> Add Existing</strong> button and in the <strong>Select UAF element</strong> dialog select the elements.</li><li><p>Assign one or more Standards or Protocols to each row that the particular UAF element must conform to by clicking the <strong>…</strong> button on any cell in the <strong>Standard/Policy</strong> column.</p></li></ul>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272738699 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02638: (2026x) Creating Actual Measurements table

- page_id: `272738699`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272738699/2026x+Creating+Actual+Measurements+table
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:50:28.975+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) UAF 1.3 modeling > (2026x) Procedures > (2026x) Measurements
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Notes

1776917953

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Notes

1776917964

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Notes

1776917954

Before creating the Actual Measurements table, a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Working with Typical Measurements table' space=''] must be created.

To create an Actual Measurements table

- Click the Add New button to add a new measurable resource. Select one or more [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Property Set elements for measurement tables' space=''] with at least one Measurement defined (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Working with Typical Measurements table' space=''] ) from the Select UAF Element dialog. The selected Property set is instantiated and the Actual Property Set is created (or the specialized instance is displayed in the table). Specify the values of each Actual Measurement in the table cells.
- Click the Add Existing button to add the existing Actual Measurement Set or Property Sets. Select the existing Actual Measurement Sets or Property Sets from the Select UAF Element dialog.
- Click the Add Missing Measurements button to add the missing Actual Measurements in the table. The missing slots are created for the defined measurements.

#### NOTE: Notes

Notes

- You can additionally show the Actual Property Set column in the table.
- The created instances are stored in the same location as the Actual Measurements table.

1776917943

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Property Set elements for measurement tables' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d62b5461-13a2-4845-935a-ed00d679cb32"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1776917953</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="421c27aa-ff78-415c-9fd4-b462d47d007b"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1776917964</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="05f68c40-c62d-4221-a07d-ed567d845a34"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1776917954</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Before creating the Actual Measurements table, a <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Working with Typical Measurements table" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Typical Measurements table]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> must be created.</p><p>To create an Actual Measurements table</p><hr /><ul><li>Click the <strong>Add New</strong> button to add a new measurable resource. Select one or more <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Property Set elements for measurement tables" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Property Sets]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> with at least one<span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"> Measurement</span> defined (see <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Working with Typical Measurements table" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Typical Measurements table]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>) from the <strong>Select UAF Element</strong> dialog. The selected Property set is instantiated and the Actual Property Set is created (or the specialized instance is displayed in the table).  <span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Specify the values of each Actual Measurement in the table cells.</span></li><li>Click the <strong>Add Existing</strong> button to add the existing<span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"> Actual Measurement Set </span>or Property Sets. Select the existing <span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Actual Measurement Sets </span>or Property Sets from the <strong>Select UAF Element</strong> dialog.</li><li>Click the <strong>Add Missing Measurements </strong>button to add the missing Actual Measurements in the table. The missing slots are created for the defined measurements.</li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6b345cd1-f0ba-49cf-91ee-bfe36e430935"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Notes</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>You can additionally show the Actual Property Set column in the table.</li><li>The created instances are stored in the same location as the Actual Measurements table.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ea2c3b77-25ea-4972-bda2-8b0e0a3499e8"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1776917943</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Property Set elements for measurement tables" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272731923 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02639: (2026x) Creating AcV-1 diagram

- page_id: `272731923`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272731923/2026x+Creating+AcV-1+diagram
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:39:50.429+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) UAF 1.3 modeling > (2026x) MODAF > (2026x) MODAF Acquisition Viewpoint > (2026x) MODAF. AcV-1 Acquisition Clusters
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

It is recommended to create an OV-4 before creating an AcV-1.

To create an AcV-1 diagram

1. Create Actual Projects.
2. Associate Actual Projects by dragging one project (part) to another (whole). Alternatively you can specify the whole and part properties.
3. Associate Actual Projects with Actual Organizations (OV-4) or Actual Posts (OV-4) responsible for them by using the Responsible For.

You can nest Actual Projects, Actual Organizations, and Actual Posts to one another in the diagram to make it look more attractive and easier to read.

You can also use an AcV-1 Responsibility Matrix to define the association between Actual Projects and Actual Organizational resources.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>It is recommended to create an OV-4 before creating an AcV-1.</p><p><br /></p><p>To create an AcV-1 diagram</p><hr /><ol><li>Create Actual Projects.</li><li>Associate Actual Projects by dragging one project (part) to another (whole). Alternatively you can specify the whole and part properties.</li><li>Associate Actual Projects with Actual Organizations (OV-4) or Actual Posts (OV-4) responsible for them by using the Responsible For.</li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="604b1f2d-c20b-41c3-a733-45483cde06c8"><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can nest Actual Projects, Actual Organizations, and Actual Posts to one another in the diagram to make it look more attractive and easier to read.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c9444da6-5f6c-432d-899d-0451dc051699"><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can also use an AcV-1 Responsibility Matrix to define the association between Actual Projects and Actual Organizational resources.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272730340 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02641: (2026x) Creating an aspect diagram

- page_id: `272730340`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272730340/2026x+Creating+an+aspect+diagram
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:37:02.134+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) General modeling features > (2026x) Diagramming > (2026x) Diagram aspects
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Before creating an aspect diagram, the following conditions must be met:

- The custom aspects are created in the project. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating custom aspects' space='']>]]> If you have [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) SysML v1 modeling' space=''] installed, the predefined aspect diagrams already exist in your project. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Finding predefined diagram aspects' space='']Predefined diagram aspects >>
- The Use Aspects option is enabled. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Enabling or disabling diagram aspects option' space='']How to enable the diagram aspects option >>

Creating a new aspect diagram is similar to creating any other diagram. Every way to create a diagram is described here: [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating diagrams' space='']. The difference is that aspect [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Composite Structure diagram' space=''] are automatically collected under the separate **Aspect Diagrams** group of the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating diagrams' space='']**Create Diagram** dialog. If you have SysML v1 plugin installed, the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) SysML Internal Block Diagram' space=''] with custom and predefined aspects are collected under the same **Aspect Diagrams** group.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Aspect Diagram group, highlighted in orange, with predefined and custom UML and SysML aspect diagrams. The illustration displays concepts from SysML Plugin.

**Sample model**

The model used in the figures of this page is **Diagram aspects**. To open this sample:

- Download [ATTACHMENT filename='diagram aspects.mdzip'].
- Find in the modeling tool: *<modeling tool installation directory>\samples\SysML v1\diagram aspects.mdzip.*

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

